The policeman walked up the street confidently. The impressiveness happened often and was not for show. There were few spectators. It was almost 10 o’clock at night. Chilly winds blew through the empty streets. The air had a hint of rain. The officer walks down the street. He tries doors and spins his club. He looks carefully at the busy area. His strong body and small swagger make him look good. He stands as a protector of the peace. The area had short hours for businesses and rest. Sometimes you see the lights of a cigar store. You also see lights from a lunch counter. Most of the doors are for closed businesses. The policeman walks down the block. He suddenly slows his walk. A man leans in the doorway of a dark hardware store. He has an unlit cigar in his mouth. The policeman walked up to him. The man spoke quickly. “It is okay, officer,” he said in a calm way. "I wait for a friend." It is an appointment from twenty years ago. Does it sound a bit funny to you? I can explain if you want to make sure it’s clear. A long time ago, there was a restaurant here. It was called "Big Joe" Brady’s restaurant. "Five years ago, the policeman said something." It was taken down then. The man stands in the doorway. He strikes a match and lights his cigar. The light showed a pale face with a square jaw. He had keen eyes and a small white scar. The scar was near his right eyebrow. His scarfpin has a big diamond. It is set in a strange way. "Twenty years ago, I had dinner here with my best friend. His name was Jimmy Wells, and he was the best guy." He and I grow up here in New York. We are like two brothers. We spend our time together. I was eighteen. Jimmy was twenty. The next morning, I start my journey to the West. I want to make my fortune there. You could not take Jimmy out of New York. He thinks it is the only place in the world. We agreed to meet here again in twenty years. It does not matter what happens. It does not matter how far we have to come. We thought that in twenty years, we should know our futures. We should have our lives planned and our money saved. It did not matter what they would be. "It sounds very interesting," the policeman said. "It seems there is a long time between meetings." Have you not heard from your friend since you left? "Yes, we wrote to each other for some time," said the other. "But after one or two years, we lose touch." The West is a big idea. I kept working hard on it. I know Jimmy will meet me here if he is alive. He always is the truest friend I have known. He will always remember this. I traveled a long distance to stand at this door tonight. It is worth it if my old partner comes. The man waited and took out a nice watch. The watch had small diamonds on it. "It is three minutes to ten," he said. It was ten o'clock when we said goodbye at the door. "You did well in the West, right?" The policeman asked a question. "You can count on it!" I hope Jimmy did as well. He was a slow worker. He was a good man, too. I have to compete with very smart people. I want to get my share. A man finds his rhythm in New York. The West sharpens him and makes him strong. The policeman twirled his club. He took a step or two. I am on my way. I hope your friend returns soon and is okay. Are you going to end his time quickly? I must say no! "She spoke to the other person." "I will give him at least thirty minutes." If Jimmy is alive on earth, he is here by that time. Goodbye, officer. “Good night, sir,” said the policeman. He walked along his beat. He tried doors as he went. A fine, cold drizzle falls. The wind rises into a steady blow. The few people walk quietly in that area. They pull up their coat collars and keep their hands in their pockets. At the door of the hardware store, a man waited. He came from a long way to meet an old friend. He felt uncertain and smoked his cigar. He waited for about twenty minutes. A tall man crossed the street. He wore a long coat with his collar up. He went straight to the man who waited. "Is that really you, Bob?" He asked with doubt. "Is that really you, Jimmy Wells?" "Help!" shouted the man at the door. "That really touches me." The new arrival shouted. He held both the other’s hands tightly. "It is Bob, and I am sure of that." I was sure I would find you here. I hoped you were still alive. "Well, well, well!" Twenty years feels like a very long time. The old restaurant is gone. I wish it had stayed open. We could have had another dinner there. "How does the West treat you, old man?" "Bully has given me everything I wanted." You have changed a lot, Jimmy. I never thought you were so tall. You are two or three inches taller. I grew a little after I turned twenty. "Are you doing well in New York, Jimmy?" "Some things are okay, but some things are not." I work in a city department. Come on, Bob. We will go to a place I know. We can have a good talk about old times. The two men walk up the street together. The man from the West feels proud of his success. He starts to tell the story of his career. The other person was under his coat. He listened with interest. A drug store stood at the corner. It shone with bright electric lights. When they walked into the light, they turned to look at each other. The man from the West stopped. He released his arm. "You are not Jimmy Wells," he said sharply. Twenty years is a long time. It is not long enough to change a man’s nose. "It sometimes changes a good man into a bad man," said the tall man. "You are under arrest for ten minutes, ‘Silky’ Bob." Chicago thinks you came our way and wants to talk to you. "Are you going quietly?" That is a good idea. Now, before we go to the station, I have a note for you. You can read it here by the window. It is from Officer Wells. The man from the West took the small paper. He opened the paper that someone gave him. His hand is steady when he starts to read. It shakes a little when he finishes. The note is very short. Bob was at the right place. He arrived on time. When you struck the match to light your cigar, I saw a man. He was the one wanted in Chicago. I could not do it myself. I found a plainclothes officer to help me. Jimmy.
